title: practical english usage - Part 175
content:
'Let's go for a walk, shall we? ' Yes, let's.' can be used as a short answer.

Shall we? is used as a question tag (►  305-306) in British English for Let's; let's Let us pray. Let's have a drink. OK,  let's all get moving.

orders to a group that includes the speaker.

We   can use let us (formal) or let's (informal) to make suggestions or to give 1  first    person     plural     imperative:       let's    ...

the hearer). However, let can be used to construct a type of imperative.

should do something), or a third-person imperative (for other people besides English verbs do not have a first-person imperative (used to suggest that I or we 225 let    introducing        imperatives         ___ Never speak to me like that again.

Always remember what I told you. (     Remember always ...) Always and never come  before imperatives.

7 word    order     with    always        and   never Don't tell anybody, will you?

After negative imperatives, will you? is used.

Be quiet, can't you? Sit down, won't you?

Can't you and won't you are more emphatic.

Get me something to drink, can you?

Give me a hand, will you?  Wait here for a minute, would you?

can you? and could you?

After imperatives, common question tags (►   305-306) are will you?, would you?, 6 question       tags not Don't anybody say a word. (     Anybody don't say...) not   ou Don't you believe it. (     Y  don't believe it.) Note the word order in negative imperatives with pronoun subjects.

You take your hands off me!

You just sit down and relax for a bit.

You before an imperative can suggest emphatic persuasion or anger.

Somebody answer the phone. Relax, everybody.

Emily come here - everybody else stay where you are.

pronoun to make it clear who  we  are speaking to.

The  imperative does not usually have a subject, but we  can use a noun or 5 subject      with    imperative:       Somebody           answer        the    phone.

grammar •  225  let introducing imperatives Let there be no doubt in your minds about our intentions.

Note the structure with let + the infinitive of there is.

’ Your boyfriend's going out with another girl.’ ’ Let him. I don’t care.’ Let our enemies understand that we will not hesitate to defend our territory.

Let the prayers begin.

informal uses are also possible.

the speaker or hearer. This is common in formal and ceremonial language, but Let can also introduce a suggestion or order for someone or something else, not 3 third-person        imperative:       let    him     ...

Let’s see. Suppose I take the M6 ...

In a very informal style, let’s is often used to mean let me (also ►     174.6).

Let me just get my coat and I'll be with you.

What’s the best way to Manchester? Let me see - suppose I take the M6.

’ What time shall we leave? ’ Let me think. Yes, eight o’clock will be OK. ’ me think are very common.

Let me is used to ‘ give instructions to oneself; the expressions Let me see and Let 2 first    person     singular      imperative:       let    me   ...

Basic Clause Types    Section  19 grammar •  Section 20  Conjunctions, Sentences and Clauses Examples of co-ordinating conjunctions: and, but, either... or.

The forecast was impressive but the results were disappointing.

Dan cooked supper and Joe washed the dishes.

Co-ordinate clauses have equal weight: neither is subordinate to the other.

co-ordinate        clauses in case.

Some conjunctions are made up of two or more words, for example as soon as, Examples of subordinating conjunctions: when, after, how, that.

a    storythat made me laugh out loud.

She told me a  funny story, Relative clauses function rather like adjectives in noun phrases: as fast as we could.

We drove home at high speed, Adverbial clauses function rather like adverbials: that I knew nothing about it.

/ told them a lie.

Noun clauses function rather like noun phrases: main groups: noun clauses, adverbial clauses and adjective (relative) clauses.

adverbial.
